dominant_error_pattern: Student treats cross-battery disagreement (e.g., average WISC-V FR, low NEPSY-II problem-solving) as grounds for global test invalidity, discarding interpretable task-specific variance.
instructional_red_flags: Normalize cross-battery variance: both batteries measure overlapping constructs but differ in task format, pacing, and ecological demand. Present a profile: WISC-V MR average, NEPSY Visual Puzzles low. Ask: "Invalid or informative?" Walk through: task differences (speed pressure, visual load, feedback) explain variance. Teach students to parse context, not assume blanket invalidity.

dominant_error_pattern: Student maps digit-span recall primarily to processing speed rather than the capacity to hold and manipulate information in working memory.
instructional_red_flags: Address with a motor-speed manipulation task: administer digit span twice—once at normal pace, once ultra-slow. Scores stay consistent, proving speed ≠ the limiting factor. Then compare to true PSI (symbol-coding) where pace heavily influences performance, illustrating the dissociation.

dominant_error_pattern: Student selects intensive processing-intervention targets based on average processing scores in unrelated domains or high scores in areas orthogonal to the academic concern.
instructional_red_flags: Build a matching matrix: left column lists academic deficits (low reading fluency, low math accuracy); right column lists processing profiles. Ask student to match each academic weakness to the processing deficit that most directly causes it (RAN→fluency, WM→math). Show that a high score in an unrelated domain (good VC, poor math achievement) does NOT warrant VC-focused intervention.

dominant_error_pattern: Student collapses rapid-automatized naming (speed of lexical retrieval) with phoneme manipulation and auditory discrimination.
instructional_red_flags: Create a three-way contrast task: (1) Say if two words start with the same sound [discrimination]; (2) Say /c/ /a/ /t/ in order [sequencing]; (3) Name pictures as fast as possible [RAN]. Use concrete examples and timing differences to illustrate why RAN is retrieval-speed specific, not phonological awareness.

dominant_error_pattern: Student confuses a comprehensive memory battery with reasoning or achievement composites, misidentifying TOMAL-2's primary construct focus.
instructional_red_flags: Prompt a side-by-side taxonomy walk: memory batteries (TOMAL-2, WMS) vs. reasoning (WISC Fluid Reasoning, Matrix Reasoning) vs. achievement (WJ-IV ACH). Ask student to categorize 10 real subtests (e.g., Paired Associates, Matrix Reasoning, Reading Fluency) into bins, then check answers against manuals.

dominant_error_pattern: Student selects Processing Speed Index for visual-spatial reasoning assessment, inverting the cognitive domain that best aligns with geometry and spatial tasks.
instructional_red_flags: Build a processing-domain reference card linking WISC-V indices to math constructs: PRI/FR (visuospatial, geometry) vs. PSI (graphomotor speed, timed output). Present a child with spatial-math weakness (drawing angles, understanding transformations). Ask: "Which index reveals the root weakness?" Then walk through why PRI > PSI for this referral.

dominant_error_pattern: Student prioritizes fine-motor or adaptive functioning assessment over phonological processing in a reading-disability evaluation, misranking processing-domain priorities.
instructional_red_flags: Use a two-column workup: left side lists reading-SLD core processing (phonology, RAN, orthographic mapping); right side lists supplemental domains (motor, behavior, adaptation). Ask student to design a reading-evaluation battery, then highlight where phonology must appear first, prior to secondary assessments.

dominant_error_pattern: Student interprets Beery VMI (motor-perceptual task) as a global intellectual-ability proxy, rather than a measure specific to integration of perception and motor output.
instructional_red_flags: Deliver a hands-on contrast: have student attempt Beery VMI copy tasks, then a WISC-V Matrix Reasoning item. Debrief: both involve visual inputs, but Beery requires motor output + perception while Matrix is purely reasoning/nonverbal. Use this lived experience to cement the distinction between construct domains.

dominant_error_pattern: Student applies PSW logic by pairing globally low cognition with uneven achievement, conflating the model's requirement for circumscribed processing-achievement inconsistency.
instructional_red_flags: Present three case profiles: (A) average Fluid Reasoning, low reading + RAN; (B) low Fluid Reasoning, low reading + RAN; (C) high Fluid Reasoning, low math + low working memory. Ask student to identify which meet PSW criteria. Emphasize that PSW requires strengths in unaffected domains, not global impairment.

dominant_error_pattern: Student misclassifies NEPSY-II as a narrow behavior-rating tool for ADHD, overlooking its comprehensive neuropsych-performance battery structure.
instructional_red_flags: Create a matrix of major batteries by class: cognitive (WISC), achievement (WJ-IV ACH), neuropsych (NEPSY), memory (TOMAL-2), behavior (CONNERS). Have student place real examples into bins, then validate against test manuals. Stress performance-based vs. informant-rating distinction.
